1 Introduction
--------------

Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM), which makes up nearly 50% of malignant brain tumors, remains the most lethal CNS malignancy, with a 5-year survival rate of less than 7% [[1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR1 "Ostrom, Q. T., Cote, D. J., Ascha, M., Kruchko, C., & Barnholtz-Sloan, J. S. (2018). Adult glioma incidence and survival by race or ethnicity in the United States from 2000 to 2014. JAMA Oncology, 4(9), 1254–1262. 
                https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2018.1789
                
              "),[2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR2 "Ostrom, Q. T., Price, M., Neff, C., Cioffi, G., Waite, K. A., Kruchko, C., & Barnholtz-Sloan, J. S. (2023). CBTRUS statistical report: Primary brain and other central nervous system tumors diagnosed in the United States in 2016-2020. Neuro-Oncology, 25(12 Suppl 2), iv1–iv99. 
                https://doi.org/10.1093/neuonc/noad149
                
              "),[3](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR3 "Schaff, L. R., & Mellinghoff, I. K. (2023). Glioblastoma and other primary brain malignancies in adults: A review. JAMA, 329(7), 574–587. 
                https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0023
                
              ")]. Despite aggressive multimodal treatment, including maximum surgical resection followed by temozolomide-based chemotherapy and radiation, tumor recurrence occurs in over 90% of patients within 7 months after treatment [[3](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR3 "Schaff, L. R., & Mellinghoff, I. K. (2023). Glioblastoma and other primary brain malignancies in adults: A review. JAMA, 329(7), 574–587. 
                https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0023
                
              "), [4](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR4 "Fukushima, C. M., & de Groot, J. (2024). Updates for newly diagnosed and recurrent glioblastoma: A review of recent clinical trials. Current Opinion in Neurology, 37(6), 666–671. 
                https://doi.org/10.1097/wco.0000000000001320
                
              ")]. Therapeutic failure is mainly attributed to the characteristics of GBM, such as infiltrative growth beyond surgical margins, chemo- and radioresistant stem-like cells, and an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment [[5](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR5 "Wu, W., Klockow, J. L., Zhang, M., Lafortune, F., Chang, E., Jin, L., Wu, Y., & Daldrup-Link, H. E. (2021). Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM): An overview of current therapies and mechanisms of resistance. Pharmacological Research, 171, Article 105780. 
                https://doi.org/10.1016/j.phrs.2021.105780
                
              "), [6](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR6 "Angom, R. S., Nakka, N. M. R., & Bhattacharya, S. (2023). Advances in glioblastoma therapy: An update on current approaches. Brain Sciences. 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/brainsci13111536
                
              ")]. These pathophysiological barriers highlight the urgent need for distinct therapeutic approaches.

5-Aminolevulinic acid-assisted photodynamic therapy (ALA-PDT) has emerged as a promising new treatment for GBM, different from traditional therapies [[7](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR7 "Stepp, H., & Stummer, W. (2018). 5-ALA in the management of malignant glioma. Lasers in Surgery and Medicine, 50(5), 399–419. 
                https://doi.org/10.1002/lsm.22933
                
              "), [8](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR8 "Sharma, S., Gone, G. B., Roychowdhury, P., Kim, H. S., Chung, S. J., Kuppusamy, G., & De, A. (2025). Photodynamic and sonodynamic therapy synergy: Mechanistic insights and cellular responses against glioblastoma multiforme. Journal of Drug Targeting, 33(4), 458–472. 
                https://doi.org/10.1080/1061186x.2024.2431676
                
              ")]. In recent years, interstitial PDT (iPDT) has emerged as a minimally invasive ALA-PDT approach that delivers light through stereotactically implanted fiber diffusers, demonstrating significant potential in treating GBM [[9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR9 "Mahmoudi, K., Garvey, K. L., Bouras, A., Cramer, G., Stepp, H., Jesu Raj, J. G., Bozec, D., Busch, T. M., & Hadjipanayis, C. G. (2019). 5-aminolevulinic acid photodynamic therapy for the treatment of high-grade gliomas. Journal of Neuro-Oncology, 141(3), 595–607. 
                https://doi.org/10.1007/s11060-019-03103-4

                https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pdpdt.2021.102492
                
              "),[11](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR11 "Quach, S., Schwartz, C., Aumiller, M., Foglar, M., Schmutzer, M., Katzendobler, S., El Fahim, M., Forbrig, R., Bochmann, K., Egensperger, R., Sroka, R., Stepp, H., Rühm, A., & Thon, N. (2023). Interstitial photodynamic therapy for newly diagnosed glioblastoma. Journal of Neuro-Oncology, 162(1), 217–223. 
                https://doi.org/10.1007/s11060-023-04284-9
                
              "),[12](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR12 "Foglar, M., Aumiller, M., Bochmann, K., Buchner, A., El Fahim, M., Quach, S., Sroka, R., Stepp, H., Thon, N., Forbrig, R., & Rühm, A. (2023). Interstitial photodynamic therapy of glioblastomas: A long-term follow-up analysis of survival and volumetric MRI data. Cancers (Basel). 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/cancers15092603
                
              "),[13](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR13 "Teng, C. W., Amirshaghaghi, A., Cho, S. S., Cai, S. S., De Ravin, E., Singh, Y., Miller, J., Sheikh, S., Delikatny, E., Cheng, Z., Busch, T. M., Dorsey, J. F., Singhal, S., Tsourkas, A., & Lee, J. Y. K. (2020). Combined fluorescence-guided surgery and photodynamic therapy for glioblastoma multiforme using cyanine and chlorin nanocluster. Journal of Neuro-Oncology. 
                https://doi.org/10.1007/s11060-020-03618-1
                
              "),[14](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR14 "Lietke, S., Schmutzer, M., Schwartz, C., Weller, J., Siller, S., Aumiller, M., Heckl, C., Forbrig, R., Niyazi, M., Egensperger, R., Stepp, H., Sroka, R., Tonn, J. C., Rühm, A., & Thon, N. (2021). Interstitial photodynamic therapy using 5-ALA for malignant glioma recurrences. Cancers (Basel). 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/cancers13081767
                
              ")]. Nevertheless, its clinical application encounters several biophysical challenges. The heterogeneity of tumors and hypoxic microenvironments restricts the absorption and distribution of ALA and the synthesis of PpIX [[8](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR8 "Sharma, S., Gone, G. B., Roychowdhury, P., Kim, H. S., Chung, S. J., Kuppusamy, G., & De, A. (2025). Photodynamic and sonodynamic therapy synergy: Mechanistic insights and cellular responses against glioblastoma multiforme. Journal of Drug Targeting, 33(4), 458–472. 
                https://doi.org/10.1080/1061186x.2024.2431676
                
              "), [15](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR15 "Leroy, H. A., Vermandel, M., Vignion-Dewalle, A. S., Leroux, B., Maurage, C. A., Duhamel, A., Mordon, S., & Reyns, N. (2017). Interstitial photodynamic therapy and glioblastoma: Light fractionation in a preclinical model. Lasers in Surgery and Medicine, 49(5), 506–515. 
                https://doi.org/10.1002/lsm.22620
                
              ")]. Additionally, in vivo experiments have demonstrated that inherent tissue heterogeneities lead to distortions in the light profile. This issue compounds the non-uniform light profile produced by the diffuser at short distances from its surface during iPDT, which may have negative consequences for iPDT [[16](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR16 "Stringasci, M. D., Fortunato, T. C., Moriyama, L. T., Filho, J. D. V., Bagnato, V. S., & Kurachi, C. (2017). Interstitial PDT using diffuser fiber-investigation in phantom and in vivo models. Lasers in Medical Science, 32(5), 1009–1016. 
                https://doi.org/10.1007/s10103-017-2225-7
                
              "), [17](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR17 "Traylor, J. I., Pernik, M. N., Sternisha, A. C., McBrayer, S. K., & Abdullah, K. G. (2021). Molecular and metabolic mechanisms underlying selective 5-aminolevulinic acid-induced fluorescence in gliomas. Cancers (Basel). 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/cancers13030580
                
              ")]. These unavoidable factors necessitate combined strategies to enhance PDT efficacy, and some medicines and molecules that address PDT’s challenges deserve further investigation.

Methadone, a synthetic opioid with analgesic properties, has recently gained attention for its potential antitumor and chemosensitizing effects, although this remains controversial [[18](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR18 "Friesen, C., Hormann, I., Roscher, M., Fichtner, I., Alt, A., Hilger, R., Debatin, K. M., & Miltner, E. (2014). Opioid receptor activation, triggering downregulation of cAMP, improves the effectiveness of anti-cancer drugs in the treatment of glioblastoma. Cell Cycle, 13(10), 1560–1570. 
                https://doi.org/10.4161/cc.28493
                
              "),[19](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR19 "Brawanski, K., Brockhoff, G., Hau, P., Vollmann-Zwerenz, A., Freyschlag, C., Lohmeier, A., Riemenschneider, M. J., Thome, C., Brawanski, A., & Proescholdt, M. A. (2018). Efficacy of D, L-methadone in the treatment of glioblastoma in vitro. CNS Oncology, 7(3), Article CNS18. 
                https://doi.org/10.2217/cns-2018-0006
                
              "),[20](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR20 "Vatter, T., Klumpp, L., Ganser, K., Stransky, N., Zips, D., Eckert, F., & Huber, S. M. (2020). Against repurposing methadone for glioblastoma therapy. Biomolecules. 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/biom10060917
                
              ")]. Preclinical studies report conflicting results. In the context of chemotherapy, some studies suggest that methadone enhances chemotherapy-induced apoptosis in leukemia and GBM cells through μ-opioid receptor (MOR)-dependent pathways or by inhibiting P-glycoprotein [[18](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR18 "Friesen, C., Hormann, I., Roscher, M., Fichtner, I., Alt, A., Hilger, R., Debatin, K. M., & Miltner, E. (2014). Opioid receptor activation, triggering downregulation of cAMP, improves the effectiveness of anti-cancer drugs in the treatment of glioblastoma. Cell Cycle, 13(10), 1560–1570. 
                https://doi.org/10.4161/cc.28493
                
              "), [19](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR19 "Brawanski, K., Brockhoff, G., Hau, P., Vollmann-Zwerenz, A., Freyschlag, C., Lohmeier, A., Riemenschneider, M. J., Thome, C., Brawanski, A., & Proescholdt, M. A. (2018). Efficacy of D, L-methadone in the treatment of glioblastoma in vitro. CNS Oncology, 7(3), Article CNS18. 
                https://doi.org/10.2217/cns-2018-0006
                
              ")]. However, more studies demonstrate that there are no synergistic effects between TMZ and radiation in vitro at safe concentrations, and the sensitizing effect observed only at cytotoxic concentrations is drug- and cell line-dependent and independent of the MOR pathway [[20](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR20 "Vatter, T., Klumpp, L., Ganser, K., Stransky, N., Zips, D., Eckert, F., & Huber, S. M. (2020). Against repurposing methadone for glioblastoma therapy. Biomolecules. 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/biom10060917
                
              "), [21](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR21 "Oppermann, H., Matusova, M., Glasow, A., Dietterle, J., Baran-Schmidt, R., Neumann, K., Meixensberger, J., & Gaunitz, F. (2019). D, L-methadone does not improve the efficacy of radio- and chemotherapy in vitro for glioblastoma. Cancer Chemotherapy and Pharmacology, 83(6), 1017–1024. 
                https://doi.org/10.1007/s00280-019-03816-3
                
              ")]. Conversely, new evidence emphasizes the role of methadone in enhancing non-chemotherapeutic treatments. Shi et al. demonstrated that methadone significantly enhanced ALA-PDT efficacy in A172 GBM cells by promoting apoptosis and G0/G1 cell cycle arrest, despite inconsistent effects on PpIX accumulation [[22](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR22 "Shi, L., Buchner, A., Pohla, H., Pongratz, T., Rühm, A., Zimmermann, W., Gederaas, O. A., Zhang, L., Wang, X., Stepp, H., & Sroka, R. (2019). Methadone enhances the effectiveness of 5-aminolevulinic acid-based photodynamic therapy for squamous cell carcinoma and glioblastoma in vitro. Journal of Biophotonics, 12(10), Article e201800468. 
                https://doi.org/10.1002/jbio.201800468
                
              ")]. These context-dependent discrepancies suggest that the sensitizing potential of methadone may critically depend on the therapeutic setting, such as chemotherapy drugs or PDT, and on different cell lines. Due to the heterogeneity of GBM and the conflicting results reported across studies, validation in multiple cell lines is crucial to establish generalizability and clarify the underlying mechanisms. This study aims to investigate further the sensitizing effects of methadone on ALA-PDT across multiple GBM cell lines (A172, U251, U87) using dose–response analyses. Technically, sublethal-dose ALA-PDT in vitro was used to mimic the limitations of PDT, such as limited ALA intake and insufficient light irradiation for GBM tumors, in combination with methadone at two concentrations.

2 Materials and methods
-----------------------

### 2.1 Cell lines and cell culture

GBM cell lines were involved in the experiments: four established cell lines (A172, U251, U87, U373, ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA) and one primary cell line (GB14, LIFE Center, University Hospital, LMU). There are other cell lines: a human chronic myelogenous leukemia cell line, K562, and a human hypopharyngeal squamous cell line, FaDu (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA). All cell lines were cultured in DMEM (4.5 g glucose/ml, Gibco, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Germany), supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Biochrom, Berlin, Germany), antibiotics (100 U/ml penicillin, 100 μg/ml streptomycin, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), and MEM non-essential amino acids (Gibco, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Germany). The cells were cultivated in an incubator at 37˚C with 5% CO 2 and passaged with 0.05% trypsin/0.02% EDTA every 2–3 days. The cells were seeded in 96-well plates at densities of 5 × 10 3, 2.5 × 10 3, and 5 × 10 3 cells per well (A172, U251, GB14) and in 6-well plates at densities of 5 × 10 5, 2.5 × 10 5, and 5 × 10 5 cells per well (A172, U251, GB14).

### 2.2 Measurement of the μ-opioid receptor (MOR) in GBM cell lines

GBM cell lines (A172, U87, U251, U373) and two positive control cell lines (K562 and FADU) were included in this MOR assay. Cells were harvested into flow cytometry tubes. After two washes with 1%bovine serum albumin(BSA) in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), BSA/PBS solution, the cells were resuspended in 100 µl BSA/PBS solution and incubated for 20 min, and then treated with 5 µl OPRM1 Polyclonal Antibody (PA5-26138, Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA) for 1 h. After washing with BSA/PBS solution, the cells were resuspended in 250 µl BSA/PBS solution and incubated with 1 µl Goat anti-Rabbit IgG Cross-Adsorbed Secondary Antibody (FITC conjugate) (F-2765, Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA) for 1 h. Following a final wash with BSA/PBS, MOR levels were measured using a flow cytometer (FACS Calibur, BD, Heidelberg, Germany). Three groups were included for each cell line: cells only group, cells + Secondary Antibody/FITC (control group), and cells + antibody + Secondary Antibody/FITC (experiment group). All cell treatments were performed on ice in a dark room. Fluorescence was excited at 488 nm, detected with a 530 nm filter, and measured by the FL1 photomultiplier tube of the flow cytometer. The mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) was calculated using the software FlowJo v10 Workspace (FlowJo, LLC, OR, USA). Relative fluorescence intensity (RFI) represents the fold change in MOR levels. RFI = (MFI of experimental group—MFI of cells only group) / (MFI of control group—MFI of cells only group).

### 2.3 Cytotoxicity measurement of methadone

D, L-methadone hydrochloride powder (M0267, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) was dissolved in 96% ethanol and aliquoted for storage at room temperature. The stock solution (5 ~ 40 μg/ml) with DMEM ± FBS (complete medium, CM, or serum-free medium, SFM) was stored at 4℃ for less than 1 week and wrapped in aluminum foil. The cells were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated with methadone at various concentrations (final concentrations of 2.5, 5, 10, and 20 μg/ml). The medium was renewed after 48 h with SFM, consistent with the apoptosis procedure. After 72 h, cytotoxicity was assessed using the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8, 96,992, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) on the ELISA Microplate Reader (450 nm, Molecular Devices, San Jose, CA, USA).

### 2.4 Sublethal-dose ALA-PDT parameters

According to previous pre-experiments [[22](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR22 "Shi, L., Buchner, A., Pohla, H., Pongratz, T., Rühm, A., Zimmermann, W., Gederaas, O. A., Zhang, L., Wang, X., Stepp, H., & Sroka, R. (2019). Methadone enhances the effectiveness of 5-aminolevulinic acid-based photodynamic therapy for squamous cell carcinoma and glioblastoma in vitro. Journal of Biophotonics, 12(10), Article e201800468. 
                https://doi.org/10.1002/jbio.201800468
                
              ")], the sublethal-dose ALA-PDT was defined as a 20%-40% reduction in viability 24 h post-PDT. The sublethal-dose ALA-PDT parameter varies depending on cell lines (A172, 1 mM ALA, 100 mW/cm 2, 1.2 J/cm 2; U251, 0.5 mM ALA, 100 mW/cm 2, 1 J/cm 2; GB14, 0.5 mM ALA, 100 mW/cm 2, 0.5 J/cm 2). The ALA-PDT parameter was utilized throughout the experiment. ALA (number, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) was dissolved in SFM for 24 h of incubation with cells. ALA solutions were freshly prepared each time. Cells were irradiated with a laser irradiation chamber (600 μm fiber with custom-made projection optics connected to a 635 nm Ceralas diode laser, CeramOptec GmbH, Bonn, Germany) [[23](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR23 "Knappe, A., Beyer, W., Riesenberg, R., Schneede, P., Sroka, R., Unsöld, E., & Valet, G. (1995). [Computer-controlled laser irradiation unit for studies of light-induced processes in cell cultures]. Biomedical Engineering = Biomedizinische Technik, 40(10), 272–275. 
                https://doi.org/10.1515/bmte.1995.40.10.272
                
              . (Computergesteuerte Laserbestrahlungseinheit für Untersuchungen lichtinduzierter Prozesse an Zellkulturen.).")].

### 2.5 Cell viability assay

Six groups were included in the cell viability assay: the untreated cells group (UC), the methadone 10 µg/ml only group (MET10), the ALA-PDT group (PDT), the methadone plus ALA-PDT group (PDT + MET 2.5 µg/ml, PDT + MET 10 µg/mL), and the ALA-PDT plus methadone 10 µg/ml plus naloxone 0.0213 µg/ml (0.065 μM) (PDT + MET 10 + NAL). The cells were seeded in 96-well plates. After 48 h, the medium was replaced with SFM, and ALA was incubated for an additional 24 h before irradiation, as described above (paragraph 2.4). Following irradiation, the SFM was renewed immediately to complete the medium. After another 24 h, cell viability was assessed using CCK8 (as paragraph 2.3).

### 2.6 Apoptosis assay

Cell apoptosis was assessed using an Annexin V-APC/7-AAD apoptosis detection kit (BD Biosciences, Germany) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Six groups were included in the apoptosis assay, as in the cell viability assay. The cells were seeded in 6-well plates. The cells were collected into flow cytometry tubes 24 h after irradiation, washed twice with 2 ml PBS, and resuspended in 100 μl of Annexin V buffer containing 5 μl Annexin V-APC and 5 μl 7-AAD for 15 min on ice in the dark. Following this, 100 μl of 1 × BD buffer was added before the assay. The apoptosis rate was determined using flow cytometry (FACS Calibur, BD, Heidelberg, Germany). Annexin V-APC and 7-AAD fluorescence were excited at 633 nm and 488 nm, respectively, with detection at 670 nm. The cell populations were analyzed as follows: viable cells (Annexin V-APC-/7-AAD-), early apoptotic cells (Annexin V-APC + /7-AAD-), and late apoptotic/necrotic cells (Annexin V-APC + /7-AAD +). The total apoptotic cell percentage was the sum of the early- and late-apoptotic populations. All apoptosis experiments were conducted in triplicate across five independent assays.

### 2.7 PpIX fluorescence assay after methadone incubation with ALA

Four groups were designed for PpIX assays: the untreated cells group (UC), the methadone-only group (MET), the ALA-only group (ALA), and the methadone plus ALA group (MET + ALA). The cell lines were seeded in 6-well plates with or without methadone (10 μg/ml) for 72 h. After 48 h of incubation, the SFM was renewed in parallel with or without ALA (ALA dose is consistent with the sublethal-dose ALA-PDT parameter in this experimental system, A172, 1 mM; U251, 0.5 mM; GB14, 1 mM). PpIX fluorescence was measured using flow cytometry (FACS Calibur, BD, Germany) at an excitation wavelength of 633 nm with detection at a wavelength of 670 nm. The Geometric Mean Fluorescence Intensity (MFI) of PpIX fluorescence was calculated using FlowJo v10 workspace (FlowJo LLC, OR, USA). All PpIX experiments were performed in triplicate across three independent assays.

### 2.8 Reactive oxygen species (ROS) assay

Four groups were designed in the ROS assay: the untreated cells group (UC), the methadone only group (MET), the ALA-PDT-only group (PDT), and the methadone plus ALA-PDT group (MET + PDT). The intracellular ROS generation was measured using the DCFDA Cellular ROS Detection Assay Kit (ab113851, Abcam, UK). DCFDA is a fluorogenic dye that detects hydroxyl, peroxyl, and other ROS activities within cells. When ROS are present, this compound is oxidized within cells, producing the fluorescent compound 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF), which remains within cells. The cell lines were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated with methadone (10 μg/ml). After 48 h, the SFM was renewed, and ALA was incubated for an additional 24 h. Twenty micromolar DCFDA was added to the wells 10 min before irradiation. After irradiation, cells were incubated in medium containing 20 μM DCFDA for 90 min at 37°C in an incubator. The ROS fluorescence was then assayed using a microplate reader (Fluostar Optima, BMG LabTech, Ortenberg, Germany) at an excitation wavelength of 685 nm, with detection at 535 nm. All experiments were performed in triplicate across three independent assays (see Fig.[1](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig1)).

### 2.9 Statistical method

Each assay was performed in 3 to 5 duplicates, and three independent assays were conducted (n = 3 or 5). Statistical tests were conducted using SPSS 13.0 software (IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY, USA). The One-way ANOVA Test was used to compare the survival rates, PpIX fluorescence, apoptosis rates, and ROS between different groups. P-values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

3 Results
---------

### 3.1 MOR expression of GBM cell lines

The relative fluorescence intensity (RFI) of MOR in K562, FADU (positive control cell lines), A172, U87, U251, and U373 cell lines was evaluated to investigate the expression levels of MOR in various cell lines (Fig.[2](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig2)). In the K562 cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 6.77 ± 0.39, significantly higher compared to the other cell lines (p < 0.0001). In the FADU cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 4.56 ± 0.21, also substantially higher than the other cell lines (all p < 0.0001). In the A172 cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 1.22 ± 0.01, which was significantly lower compared to the K562 and FADU cell lines (p < 0.0001). In the U251 cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 1.54 ± 0.35, significantly lower than that of the K562 and FADU cell lines (p < 0.0001), but not substantially different from the A172 cell line (p = 0.2296). In the U87 cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 1.46 ± 0.28, considerably lower than that of the K562 and FADU cell lines (p < 0.0001), but not significantly different from the A172 cell line and U251 cell lines (U87 vs A172, p = 0.8569; U87 vs U251, p = 0.8503). In the U373 cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 1.68 ± 0.19, considerably lower than that of the K562 and FADU cell lines (p < 0.0001), but not significantly different from the A172, U251, and U87 cell lines (U373 vs A172, p = 0.0511; U373 vs U251, p = 0.9693; U373 vs U87, p = 0.4062;).

### 3.2 Low cytotoxicity of methadone on the three GBM cell lines

To investigate the cytotoxicity of methadone on glioblastoma cell lines, the cell viability of A172, U251, and GB14 after incubation with gradient concentrations of methadone (2.5, 5, 10, 20 μg/mL) for 72 h was evaluated using the CCK8 assay (experimental schedule details shown in Fig.[1](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig1)). In the A172 cell line, the cell viability (%) of methadone-treated groups at gradient concentrations was 96.05 ± 3.95, 93.39 ± 6.61, 93.74 ± 6.26, and 88.12 ± 11.88, compared to the untreated A172 cells (UC: untretad control) group, respectively (MET 2.5 vs UC, p = 0.8500; MET 5 vs UC, p = 0.2897, MET 10 vs UC, p = 0.0866; MET 20 vs UC, p < 0.0001). For the U251 cell line, the cell viability (%) of methadone groups was 106.80 ± 6.61, 109.20 ± 7.16, 105.60 ± 4.04, and 102.20 ± 6.61, compared to the U251 UC group, respectively (MET 2.5 vs UC, p = 0.2445; MET 5 vs UC, p = 0.0649, MET 10 vs UC, p = 0.4201; MET 20 vs UC, p = 0.9549). In the GB14 cell line, the cell viability (%) was 103.78 ± 9.76, 98.66 ± 7.08, 97.36 ± 8.07, and 97.30 ± 10.67, compared to the GB14 UC group, respectively (MET 2.5 vs UC, p = 0.9868; MET 5 vs UC, p = 0.9743, MET 10 vs UC, p = 0.8696; MET 20 vs UC, p = 0.9372). U251 and GB14 all exhibited robust viability even at a high methadone concentration (20 μg/mL). In general, methadone displayed low cytotoxicity toward the three GBM cell lines. Based on the results and our previous experiments, two methadone concentrations (2.5, 10 μg/mL) were selected for subsequent experiments (see Fig.[3](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig3)).

### 3.3 Methadone effects on the viability of GBM cell lines caused by sublethal-dose ALA-PDT

To further determine the impact on the viability of methadone on sublethal-dose PDT-induced, two concentrations (2.5, 10 μg/ml) of methadone were used on a sublethal-dose ALA-PDT basis.

In the A172 cell line, the cell viability (%) of five groups (MET, PDT, PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, PDT + MET10 + NAL) is 92.00 ± 5.43, 47.60 ± 10.31, 58.00 ± 15.60, 46.20 ± 8.70, and 51.80 ± 21.42, respectively, compared to the A172 UC group (MET vs UC, p = 0.0616; PDT vs UC, p = 0.0011; PDT + MET 2.5 vs UC, p = 0.0122; PDT + MET 10 vs UC, p = 0.0122; PDT + MET10 + NAL vs UC, p = 0.0180, all p < 0.05, except the MET group). There is no statistical significance among the PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, and PDT + MET10 + NAL groups when compared to the PDT group (PDT vs PDT + MET 2.5, p = 0.7015; PDT vs PDT + MET 10, p = 0.9927; PDT vs PDT + MET 10 + NAL, p = 0.9113), indicating that methadone does not affect the viability of A172 after sublethal-dose PDT.

In the U251 cell line, the cell viability (%) of five groups (MET, PDT, PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, PDT + MET10 + NAL) is 103 ± 13.70, 74.58 ± 2.61, 83.09 ± 6.47, 87.78 ± 8.17, and 68.97 ± 7.15, respectively, compared to the U251 UC group (all p < 0.05, except the MET group). There is no statistical significance between the PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, and PDT + MET10 + NAL groups compared to the PDT group (PDT vs PDT + MET 2.5, p = 0.5462; PDT vs PDT + MET 10, p = 0.1922; PDT vs PDT + MET 10 + NAL, p = 0.8359), indicating that methadone does not have an impact on the viability of U251 after sublethal-dose PDT.

In the GB14 cell line, the cell viability (%) of 5 groups (MET, PDT, PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, PDT + MET10 + NAL) is 87 ± 9.14, 61.20 ± 16.08, 70 ± 10.32, 55.40 ± 14.74, 58.00 ± 9.77 compared to the GB14 UC group (all p < 0.05, except MET group). This indicates that PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, and PDT + MET10 + NAL groups are not statistically significant compared to the PDT group (PDT vs PDT + MET 2.5, p = 0.6734; PDT vs PDT + MET 10, p = 0.8882; PDT vs PDT + MET 10 + NAL, p = 0.9108), which means that methadone doesn’t affect the viability of GB14 after sublethal-dose PDT (see Fig.[4](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig4)).

### 3.4 Impact of methadone on the cell death pathway

Apoptosis has been recognized as the primary mode of cell death resulting from sublethal-dose ALA-PDT. To determine whether methadone affects the PDT-induced cell death pathway, e.g., apoptosis, an apoptosis assay using annexin V-APC and 7-AAD was performed. Six groups were explored in the apoptosis assay (UC, MET10, PDT, PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, PDT + MET10 + NAL).

In the A172 cell lines, 24 h after irradiation, the proportions of apoptosis (%) were 4.39 ± 1.10, 5.27 ± 1.65, 18.69 ± 4.68, 35.55 ± 14.44, 54.36 ± 12.77, and 45.53 ± 15.23 for the six groups, respectively. It seems that 2.5 μg/mL methadone tends to improve PDT-induced apoptosis, but the difference is not significant (p = 0.9762). However, 10 μg/mL methadone significantly enhances PDT-induced apoptosis with a statistically significant difference (p = 0.0208).

In the U251 cell lines, 24 h after irradiation, the proportions of apoptosis (%) were 3.56 ± 2.96, 5.09 ± 3.08, 27.39 ± 6.31, 29.66 ± 6.42, 29.34 ± 11.58, and 27.04 ± 10.17 for the six groups, respectively. Methadone did not significantly improve PDT-induced apoptosis (PDT vs PDT + MET 2.5, p = 0.9965; PDT vs PDT + MET 10, p = 0.9983; PDT vs PDT + MET 10 + NAL, p > 0.9999, all p > 0.05).

In the GB14 cell lines, 24 h after irradiation, the proportions of apoptosis (%) were 4.99 ± 2.56, 11.22 ± 7.42, 33.13 ± 4.74, 30.57 ± 9.48, 30.42 ± 13.34, and 27.69 ± 11.75 for the six groups, respectively. Methadone did not significantly enhance PDT-induced apoptosis (PDT vs PDT + MET 2.5, p = 0.4797; PDT vs PDT + MET 10, p = 0.7871; PDT vs PDT + MET 10 + NAL, p = 0.7451, all p > 0.05).

Overall, these results showed that methadone enhanced sublethal-dose PDT-induced apoptosis in a cell line-dependent manner (see Fig.[5](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig5)).

### 3.5 Impact of methadone on PpIX formation and ROS generation in GBM cell lines

To obtain an in-depth understanding of methadone’s role in promoting PDT-induced apoptosis, it is necessary first to determine whether methadone affects the production of PpIX from ALA during the photophysical process and the generation of ROS during the photochemical reaction.

In the A172 cell line, the PpIX fluorescence values (mean ± SD, a.u.) for four groups—the untreated cells group (UC), the methadone only group (MET), the ALA only group (ALA), and the ALA plus methadone group (ALA + MET) are 15.67 ± 3.38, 21.33 ± 10.40, 346.90 ± 118.60, and 343.6 ± 106, respectively. Methadone neither generated PpIX in A172 (MET vs UC, p > 0.9999) nor promoted ALA-derived PpIX (ALA vs ALA + MET, p > 0.9999). In the U251 cell line, the PpIX fluorescence values for the UC, MET, ALA, and ALA + MET groups are 2.12 ± 0.10, 5.43 ± 1.00, 251.4 ± 43.08, and 192.7 ± 27.15, respectively. Methadone did not produce PpIX in U251 (MET vs UC, p > 0.9999) or enhance ALA-derived PpIX (ALA vs ALA + MET, p = 0.1264). In the GB14 cell line, the PpIX fluorescence values for the UC, MET, ALA, and ALA + MET groups are 19.28 ± 4.96, 22.85 ± 2.18, 416.30 ± 101.60, and 434.80 ± 134.10, respectively. Methadone did not generate PpIX in GB14 (MET vs UC, p = 0.9994) or promote ALA-derived PpIX (ALA vs ALA + MET, p = 0.9340).

Overall, in this experimental system, methadone did not generate PpIX in the three GBM cell lines or enhance ALA-derived PpIX (Fig.[6](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig6)A, B).

The production of ROS following low-dose PDT in A172, U251, and GB14 was analyzed using the DCFDA assay. ROS levels in cells treated with PDT and PDT + MET increased compared to control in A172, U251, and GB14. The comparison of the derived ROS fold of PDT versus PDT + MET was 4.39 ± 1.75 vs 3.99 ± 1.32 (p = 0.9943); 3.04 ± 0.84 vs 2.41 ± 0.06 (p = 0.3640); 1.84 ± 0.28 vs 1.48 ± 0.28 (p = 0.5478), respectively (Fig.[6](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig6)C). Thus, these experiments showed no measurable effect of additional methadone on ROS production after low-dose PDT in these cell lines.

4 Discussion
------------

Interstitial photodynamic therapy (iPDT), a minimally invasive approach that delivers light directly to brain tumors via implanted optical fibers, has shown promising results for GBM in clinical settings [[11](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR11 "Quach, S., Schwartz, C., Aumiller, M., Foglar, M., Schmutzer, M., Katzendobler, S., El Fahim, M., Forbrig, R., Bochmann, K., Egensperger, R., Sroka, R., Stepp, H., Rühm, A., & Thon, N. (2023). Interstitial photodynamic therapy for newly diagnosed glioblastoma. Journal of Neuro-Oncology, 162(1), 217–223. 
                https://doi.org/10.1007/s11060-023-04284-9
                
              "), [12](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR12 "Foglar, M., Aumiller, M., Bochmann, K., Buchner, A., El Fahim, M., Quach, S., Sroka, R., Stepp, H., Thon, N., Forbrig, R., & Rühm, A. (2023). Interstitial photodynamic therapy of glioblastomas: A long-term follow-up analysis of survival and volumetric MRI data. Cancers (Basel). 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/cancers15092603
                
              "), [14](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR14 "Lietke, S., Schmutzer, M., Schwartz, C., Weller, J., Siller, S., Aumiller, M., Heckl, C., Forbrig, R., Niyazi, M., Egensperger, R., Stepp, H., Sroka, R., Tonn, J. C., Rühm, A., & Thon, N. (2021). Interstitial photodynamic therapy using 5-ALA for malignant glioma recurrences. Cancers (Basel). 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/cancers13081767
                
              ")]. However, its efficacy is constrained by inherent biological limitations, such as heterogeneous intratumoral PpIX accumulation, hypoxia, and PpIX photobleaching [[12](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR12 "Foglar, M., Aumiller, M., Bochmann, K., Buchner, A., El Fahim, M., Quach, S., Sroka, R., Stepp, H., Thon, N., Forbrig, R., & Rühm, A. (2023). Interstitial photodynamic therapy of glioblastomas: A long-term follow-up analysis of survival and volumetric MRI data. Cancers (Basel). 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/cancers15092603
                
              "), [24](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR24 "Johansson, A., Palte, G., Schnell, O., Tonn, J. C., Herms, J., & Stepp, H. (2010). 5-Aminolevulinic acid-induced protoporphyrin IX levels in the tissue of human malignant brain tumors. Photochemistry and Photobiology, 86(6), 1373–1378. 
                https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1751-1097.2010.00799.x
                
              "),[25](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR25 "Jin, C. S., Lovell, J. F., Chen, J., & Zheng, G. (2013). Ablation of hypoxic tumors with dose-equivalent photothermal, but not photodynamic, therapy using a nanostructured porphyrin assembly. ACS Nano, 7(3), 2541–2550. 
                https://doi.org/10.1021/nn3058642
                
              "),[26](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR26 "Honda, N., Ishii, K., Kajimoto, Y., Kuroiwa, T., & Awazu, K. (2018). Determination of optical properties of human brain tumor tissues from 350 to 1000 nm to investigate the cause of false negatives in fluorescence-guided resection with 5-aminolevulinic acid. Journal of Biomedical Optics, 23(7), 1–10. 
                https://doi.org/10.1117/1.Jbo.23.7.075006
                
              "),[27](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR27 "Myrzakhmetov, B., Arnoux, P., Mordon, S., Acherar, S., Tsoy, I., & Frochot, C. (2021). Photophysical properties of protoporphyrin IX, pyropheophorbide-a, and Photofrin(®) in different conditions. Pharmaceuticals (Basel). 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/ph14020138
                
              ")]. Consequently, iPDT as a monotherapy is unlikely to overcome the profound genetic and phenotypic heterogeneity of GBM, necessitating the exploration of combinatorial strategies to enhance its therapeutic effect.

In this context, methadone has emerged as a candidate for combinatorial GBM therapy, primarily based on in vitro studies reporting its ability to enhance the cytotoxicity of chemotherapeutic agents like doxorubicin [[18](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR18 "Friesen, C., Hormann, I., Roscher, M., Fichtner, I., Alt, A., Hilger, R., Debatin, K. Cell Cycle, 13(10), 1560–1570. 
                https://doi.org/10.4161/cc.28493
                
              ")], although its clinical applicability remains controversial [[19](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR19 "Brawanski, K., Brockhoff, G., Hau, P., Vollmann-Zwerenz, A., Freyschlag, C., Lohmeier, A., Riemenschneider, M. J., Thome, C., Brawanski, A., & Proescholdt, M. A. (2018). Efficacy of D, L-methadone in the treatment of glioblastoma in vitro. CNS Oncology, 7(3), Article CNS18. 
                https://doi.org/10.2217/cns-2018-0006
                
              "), [20](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR20 "Vatter, T., Klumpp, L., Ganser, K., Stransky, N., Zips, D., Eckert, F., & Huber, S. M. (2020). Against repurposing methadone for glioblastoma therapy. Biomolecules. 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/biom10060917
                
              ")]. Building on a previous report suggesting that methadone could potentiate PDT-induced apoptosis in the A172 GBM cell line [[22](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR22 "Shi, L., Buchner, A., Pohla, H., Pongratz, T., Rühm, A., Zimmermann, W., Gederaas, O. A., Zhang, L., Wang, X., Stepp, H., & Sroka, R. (2019). Methadone enhances the effectiveness of 5-aminolevulinic acid-based photodynamic therapy for squamous cell carcinoma and glioblastoma in vitro. Journal of Biophotonics, 12(10), Article e201800468. 
                https://doi.org/10.1002/jbio.201800468
                
              ")], and in light of ongoing debate regarding its role in chemo-sensitization [[20](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR20 "Vatter, T., Klumpp, L., Ganser, K., Stransky, N., Zips, D., Eckert, F., & Huber, S. M. (2020). Against repurposing methadone for glioblastoma therapy. Biomolecules. 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/biom10060917
                
              "), [21](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR21 "Oppermann, H., Matusova, M., Glasow, A., Dietterle, J., Baran-Schmidt, R., Neumann, K., Meixensberger, J., & Gaunitz, F. (2019). D, L-methadone does not improve the efficacy of radio- and chemotherapy in vitro for glioblastoma. Cancer Chemotherapy and Pharmacology, 83(6), 1017–1024. 
                https://doi.org/10.1007/s00280-019-03816-3
                
              ")], this study aimed to rigorously evaluate methadone’s potential as a sensitizer for ALA-PDT in vitro. To ensure robust and generalizable conclusions, we optimized and standardized critical experimental parameters—including cell seeding density, methadone concentration, light dose, and sublethal-dose ALA-PDT conditions—across multiple GBM cell lines (A172, U251, GB14), thereby accounting for their distinct growth characteristics and intrinsic PDT sensitivities [[25](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR25 "Jin, C. S., Lovell, J. F., Chen, J., & Zheng, G. (2013). Ablation of hypoxic tumors with dose-equivalent photothermal, but not photodynamic, therapy using a nanostructured porphyrin assembly. ACS Nano, 7(3), 2541–2550. 
                https://doi.org/10.1021/nn3058642
                
              ")]. The sublethal-dose ALA-PDT was defined as a 20%-40% reduction in viability 24 h post-PDT, because it provides a sensitive, quantifiable window for detecting synergistic enhancement from a co-treatment. The cell line-specific parameters (e.g., GB14 requiring a lower light dose than A172) directly reflect their intrinsic differences in PpIX synthesis and photosensitivity.

Our comprehensive analysis revealed that non-cytotoxic concentrations of methadone (2.5 ~ 10 µg/ml) did not significantly alter the viability of the A172, U251, and GB14 cell lines, consistent with previous studies on A172 and U118MG [[18](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR18 "Friesen, C., Hormann, I., Roscher, M., Fichtner, I., Alt, A., Hilger, R., Debatin, K. M., & Miltner, E. (2014). Opioid receptor activation, triggering downregulation of cAMP, improves the effectiveness of anti-cancer drugs in the treatment of glioblastoma. Cell Cycle, 13(10), 1560–1570. 
                https://doi.org/10.4161/cc.28493
                
              ")]. Additionally, Methadone did not enhance the apoptosis of sublethal-dose ALA-PDT in the U251 and GB14 cell lines. Critically, methadone failed to augment two fundamental pillars of ALA-PDT action: it did not increase intracellular PpIX accumulation nor amplify the photodynamic generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in any of the tested cell lines. This absence of effect on the core photodynamic mechanism provides a plausible explanation for the general lack of sensitization observed across most models.

A notable yet isolated exception was observed in the A172 cell line, where 10 µg/ml methadone (PTD + MET 10) exhibited a moderate pro-apoptotic effect when combined with PDT, confirming the earlier single-cell line study [[22](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR22 "Shi, L., Buchner, A., Pohla, H., Pongratz, T., Rühm, A., Zimmermann, W., Gederaas, O. A., Zhang, L., Wang, X., Stepp, H., & Sroka, R. (2019). Methadone enhances the effectiveness of 5-aminolevulinic acid-based photodynamic therapy for squamous cell carcinoma and glioblastoma in vitro. Journal of Biophotonics, 12(10), Article e201800468. 
                https://doi.org/10.1002/jbio.201800468
                
              ")]. This cell line-specific result highlights the critical role of tumor heterogeneity in affecting drug response. Interestingly, this A172-specific effect occurred despite uniformly low expression of the canonical μ-opioid receptor (MOR)—the primary pharmacological target of methadone—across all tested cell lines, a finding consistent with other reports [[20](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR20 "Vatter, T., Klumpp, L., Ganser, K., Stransky, N., Zips, D., Eckert, F., & Huber, S. M. (2020). Against repurposing methadone for glioblastoma therapy. Biomolecules. 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/biom10060917
                
              ")]. This indicates that the sporadic sensitizing effect of methadone in GBM may work through MOR-independent pathways, the nature and activity of which likely vary across cellular contexts.

Results from single-cell line studies may overstate the therapeutic effect by overlooking the significant molecular heterogeneity in GBM [[28](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR28 "Dąbrowski, J. M., & Arnaut, L. G. (2015). Photodynamic therapy (PDT) of cancer: From local to systemic treatment. Photochemical & Photobiological Sciences, 14(10), 1765–1780. 
                https://doi.org/10.1039/c5pp00132c
                
              "), [29](https://doi.org/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR29 "Gordon, J. L., Brown, M. A., & Reynolds, M. M. (2018). Cell-based methods for determination of efficacy for candidate therapeutics in the clinical management of cancer. Diseases. 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/diseases6040085
                
              ")]. By demonstrating that methadone’s sensitizing potential is not universal but restricted to specific cellular contexts (e.g., A172), our findings highlight the need to validate candidate PDT adjuvants across diverse cell lines. This approach is crucial for differentiating broadly applicable therapeutic methods from those that are cell-line-specific, thereby improving the translational relevance of preclinical research.

5 Conclusion
------------

In summary, this multi-cell line in vitro study demonstrated that methadone is not a universal and reliable sensitizing agent for ALA-PDT in GBM. It fails to regulate key photodynamic processes such as PpIX accumulation and ROS generation, which may be the main reason for the overall negative results. Although a cell line-dependent pro-apoptotic interaction was observed in A172 cells, possibly mediated through atypical, non-MOR-dependent mechanisms, this effect was not consistent across different cell lines and was not related to the basic photodynamic process. Therefore, the interaction between methadone and ALA-PDT is more complex than initially hypothesized, and it is highly dependent on the specific experimental context. Given the high heterogeneity of GBM, it is expected that different cell subtypes will respond differently to the combined therapy. Consequently, the recommendation to use methadone as an adjuvant to improve ALA-PDT for GBM should be approached with caution. This study also highlights the importance of mechanism-driven, multi-cell-line validation in the search for effective PDT adjuvants.

6 Limitation
------------

MOR detection was not performed on GB14 cell lines. The time point for this experiment was determined based on previous experiments and pre-experiments. When analyzing these negative results, we should consider that the treatment time (72 h) is relatively long, which may involve complex adaptation or compensation mechanisms that could affect the experimental results.

Data availability
-----------------

No datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.

# 任务
从以下论文内容中提取信息，以 JSON 格式返回。

## 严格遵守规则：绝对不要提取 References 和 Cite This Article 部分的任何信息：

- References 和 Cite This Article 中的作者姓名、单位、联系方式
- References 和 Cite This Article 中的文章标题、DOI
- References 和 Cite This Article 中的任何其他信息
- 只提取正文内容（References 和 Cite This Article 之前的部分）


## 发表日期
提取 Issue published 或 Published Time 对应的日期。如果论文同时存在 Received、Accepted、Published 等多个日期，只取发表日期，其他日期忽略。


## DOI
提取论文的 DOI，统一使用完整 URL 格式，例如 https://doi.org/10.1234/xxx。如果原文只有编号形式如 10.1234/xxx，则补全为 https://doi.org/10.1234/xxx。


## 作者与符号规则

作者列表通常位于标题下方，例如：
Weiping Yang 1,#, Wei Xiao 2,#, Wenhao Xu 3,#, Lijun Ren 1, Xian Li 1, Junhua Yu 1, Ronghua Wang 4*

符号含义如下：

数字上标表示作者所属机构编号，对应文末的机构列表。一个作者如果同时属于多个机构，就会有多个数字，比如 3,4。# 和 † 都表示共同第一作者，意味着这几位作者对论文贡献相同，并列第一，两者含义相同，不同期刊用法不同。* 表示通讯作者，负责与期刊沟通以及接收读者问询，通常是导师或负责人。以上符号可以同时出现在一个作者名字后面。

示例：

Wenhao Xu 3,# 表示该作者来自第3机构，且是共同第一作者。

Tao Li 3,4,* 表示该作者同时来自第3和第4机构，且是通讯作者。

Wei Zhao 1,3,* 表示该作者同时来自第1和第3机构，且也是通讯作者，与 Tao Li 并列通讯作者。

Wang Fang 1,#,* 表示该作者来自第1机构，同时是共同第一作者，也是通讯作者，一个人身兼两个角色。

### 脚注说明
所有符号的具体含义以论文脚注说明为准。脚注通常没有标题，直接列出符号对应的说明文字，位置一般紧跟在作者姓名和机构列表的下方，内容通常如下：
- # These authors contributed equally to this work
- † These authors have contributed equally to this work
- * Corresponding author: email@xxx.com

通讯作者的邮箱也可能出现在文末的 Correspondence、Contact 或 *Correspondence 区域，而不是作者列表下方的脚注，需要一并查找。如果邮箱字段存在但内容为空，同样填 null。

如果脚注缺失或格式不标准，按照以下降级处理：* 默认视为通讯作者，# 和 † 默认视为共同第一作者，数字上标默认对应机构编号。如果论文完全没有任何符号标注，所有作者共享同一机构地址，则联系人取作者列表中排名第一的作者。


## 地址
机构和地理地址合并在同一字段中。地址编号与作者名称后的数字上标关联对应。例如 Lijun Ren 1，他的地址信息对应：1 Department of Breast and Thyroid Surgery, Qingdao Chengyang People's Hospital, Qingdao, 266109, China。

如果机构名称中含有斜杠，如 Rudong People's Hospital/Affiliated Rudong Hospital of Xinglin College，斜杠表示同一机构的两种称呼或并列名称，翻译时保留斜杠结构，不拆分为两个地址。


## 联系人识别规则

优先提取通讯作者，通讯作者通常用 * 标注。如果有多位通讯作者，提取排名第一的那位，例如 Jike Song 1,4* 和 Hongsheng Bi 1,2,3,4* 并列通讯作者，则提取排名靠前的 Jike Song。如果论文中没有通讯作者，则提取第一作者，第一作者是作者列表中排名第一的人。如果第一作者有多人（即共同第一作者，用 # 或 † 标注），则提取其中排名第一的那位。如果论文既没有通讯作者标注也没有第一作者信息，或者论文没有任何符号标注，则默认提取作者列表中的第一个人。


## 中国作者识别规则

**A. 姓名判断（中文拼音规则）**：支持复姓。

**B. 地址判断（中国机构）**：地址字段包含关键词 china（不区分大小写）。

**C. 组合判断规则**：同时满足姓名符合中文拼音规则且地址包含中国关键词，视为中国作者。

**D. 排除规则**：地址同时包含 "USA"、"UK"、"Japan" 等其他国家关键词以及 "China"，仍视为中国（如 "China-USA 联合研究所"）。


## 字段对应关系
corresponding_author 下的 address、email、phone 必须对应联系人这个人，不是其他作者的信息。


## 翻译要求

把 address 字段翻译并放到 address_cn 字段。每个完整地址的格式从科室→机构→城市→邮编→国家。所有地址均需翻译，包括非中国机构（如美国、日本等）也需翻译成中文。

示例：
- 四川大学华西医院心内科，成都 610041，中国
- 四川省人民医院心内科，成都 610072，中国

Abstract
--------

Radiotherapy resistance remains a major barrier to effective treatment of triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), highlighting the need to identify mechanisms driving resistance. Here, we identified SDC1 as a pivotal mediator of cancer-associated fibroblast (CAF)-induced radioresistance in breast cancer. SDC1 bound the TIM barrel domain of the glycolytic enzyme ENO1, preventing FBXW7-mediated degradation and driving aerobic glycolysis and lactate accumulation. The resulting lactate-rich microenvironment not only promoted tumor stemness but also significantly impaired the cytotoxic functions of both NK cells and CD8⁺ T cells. Pharmacologic inhibition of ENO1 or lactate export restored radiosensitivity. Targeting SDC1⁺ CAFs with the antibody-drug conjugate indatuximab ravtansine (BT062) synergized with radiotherapy in vivo, markedly reducing tumor burden, depleting stem-like tumor cells, and remodeling the immune microenvironment. These findings define a CAF metabolic program that fuels tumor stemness and rewires the immune microenvironment to confer radioresistance, supporting the therapeutic targeting of SDC1⁺ CAFs in TNBC.

# 任务
从以下论文内容中提取信息，以 JSON 格式返回。

## 严格遵守规则：绝对不要提取 References 和 Cite This Article 部分的任何信息：

- References 和 Cite This Article 中的作者姓名、单位、联系方式
- References 和 Cite This Article 中的文章标题、DOI
- References 和 Cite This Article 中的任何其他信息
- 只提取正文内容（References 和 Cite This Article 之前的部分）


## 发表日期
提取 Issue published 或 Published Time 对应的日期。如果论文同时存在 Received、Accepted、Published 等多个日期，只取发表日期，其他日期忽略。


## DOI
提取论文的 DOI，统一使用完整 URL 格式，例如 https://doi.org/10.1234/xxx。如果原文只有编号形式如 10.1234/xxx，则补全为 https://doi.org/10.1234/xxx。


## 作者与符号规则

作者列表通常位于标题下方，例如：
Weiping Yang 1,#, Wei Xiao 2,#, Wenhao Xu 3,#, Lijun Ren 1, Xian Li 1, Junhua Yu 1, Ronghua Wang 4*

符号含义如下：

数字上标表示作者所属机构编号，对应文末的机构列表。一个作者如果同时属于多个机构，就会有多个数字，比如 3,4。# 和 † 都表示共同第一作者，意味着这几位作者对论文贡献相同，并列第一，两者含义相同，不同期刊用法不同。* 表示通讯作者，负责与期刊沟通以及接收读者问询，通常是导师或负责人。以上符号可以同时出现在一个作者名字后面。

示例：

Wenhao Xu 3,# 表示该作者来自第3机构，且是共同第一作者。

Tao Li 3,4,* 表示该作者同时来自第3和第4机构，且是通讯作者。

Wei Zhao 1,3,* 表示该作者同时来自第1和第3机构，且也是通讯作者，与 Tao Li 并列通讯作者。

Wang Fang 1,#,* 表示该作者来自第1机构，同时是共同第一作者，也是通讯作者，一个人身兼两个角色。

### 脚注说明
所有符号的具体含义以论文脚注说明为准。脚注通常没有标题，直接列出符号对应的说明文字，位置一般紧跟在作者姓名和机构列表的下方，内容通常如下：
- # These authors contributed equally to this work
- † These authors have contributed equally to this work
- * Corresponding author: email@xxx.com

通讯作者的邮箱也可能出现在文末的 Correspondence、Contact 或 *Correspondence 区域，而不是作者列表下方的脚注，需要一并查找。如果邮箱字段存在但内容为空，同样填 null。

如果脚注缺失或格式不标准，按照以下降级处理：* 默认视为通讯作者，# 和 † 默认视为共同第一作者，数字上标默认对应机构编号。如果论文完全没有任何符号标注，所有作者共享同一机构地址，则联系人取作者列表中排名第一的作者。


## 地址
机构和地理地址合并在同一字段中。地址编号与作者名称后的数字上标关联对应。例如 Lijun Ren 1，他的地址信息对应：1 Department of Breast and Thyroid Surgery, Qingdao Chengyang People's Hospital, Qingdao, 266109, China。

如果机构名称中含有斜杠，如 Rudong People's Hospital/Affiliated Rudong Hospital of Xinglin College，斜杠表示同一机构的两种称呼或并列名称，翻译时保留斜杠结构，不拆分为两个地址。


## 联系人识别规则

优先提取通讯作者，通讯作者通常用 * 标注。如果有多位通讯作者，提取排名第一的那位，例如 Jike Song 1,4* 和 Hongsheng Bi 1,2,3,4* 并列通讯作者，则提取排名靠前的 Jike Song。如果论文中没有通讯作者，则提取第一作者，第一作者是作者列表中排名第一的人。如果第一作者有多人（即共同第一作者，用 # 或 † 标注），则提取其中排名第一的那位。如果论文既没有通讯作者标注也没有第一作者信息，或者论文没有任何符号标注，则默认提取作者列表中的第一个人。


## 中国作者识别规则

**A. 姓名判断（中文拼音规则）**：支持复姓。

**B. 地址判断（中国机构）**：地址字段包含关键词 china（不区分大小写）。

**C. 组合判断规则**：同时满足姓名符合中文拼音规则且地址包含中国关键词，视为中国作者。

**D. 排除规则**：地址同时包含 "USA"、"UK"、"Japan" 等其他国家关键词以及 "China"，仍视为中国（如 "China-USA 联合研究所"）。


## 字段对应关系
corresponding_author 下的 address、email、phone 必须对应联系人这个人，不是其他作者的信息。


## 翻译要求

把 address 字段翻译并放到 address_cn 字段。每个完整地址的格式从科室→机构→城市→邮编→国家。所有地址均需翻译，包括非中国机构（如美国、日本等）也需翻译成中文。

示例：
- 四川大学华西医院心内科，成都 610041，中国
- 四川省人民医院心内科，成都 610072，中国

Construction and validation of a palmitoylation-related prognostic model for lung adenocarcinoma based on integrated bioinformatics and machine learning
===============
[![Image 2: A 120x26 small image, likely a logo, icon or avatar](https://cdn.amegroups.cn/static/mirror/crossmark/widget/v2.0/CROSSMARK_Color_horizontal.svg)](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389)

Original Article

Construction and validation of a palmitoylation-related prognostic model for lung adenocarcinoma based on integrated bioinformatics and machine learning
========================================================================================================================================================

Zhilan Huang 1#[![Image 3: ORCID logo](https://info.orcid.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/11/orcid_16x16.png)](https://orcid.org/0009-0008-9748-9186), Tingyi Xie 1#, Mingwen Tang 2, Anqi Su 1, Zhujin Jin 1, Zhuni Chen 2, Dan Jia 2, Wei Xie 2[![Image 4: ORCID logo](https://info.orcid.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/11/orcid_16x16.png)](https://orcid.org/0009-0005-0909-5557)

1 The Fourth Clinical Medical College of Guangzhou University of Chinese Medicine, Shenzhen, China; 2 Department of Respiratory Medicine, Shenzhen Traditional Chinese Medicine Hospital, Shenzhen, China

_Contributions:_ (I) Conception and design: Z Huang, T Xie, M Tang, W Xie; (II) Administrative support: W Xie; (III) Provision of study materials or patients: None; (IV) Collection and assembly of data: Z Huang, T Xie, Z Chen; (V) Data analysis and interpretation: Z Huang, M Tang, D Jia, A Su; (VI) Manuscript writing: All authors; (VII) Final approval of manuscript: All authors.

#These authors contributed equally to this work.

_Correspondence to:_ Wei Xie, PhD. Department of Respiratory Medicine, Shenzhen Traditional Chinese Medicine Hospital, No. 1 Fuhua Road, Futian District, Shenzhen 518033, China. Email: xiew0703@163.com.

* * *

**Background:**Lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) is a prevalent malignancy whose therapeutic management is complicated by nonspecific early symptoms, late-stage diagnosis, and aggressive metastasis. Given the critical role of palmitoylation in LUAD progression, this study aims to construct a prognostic model based on palmitoylation-related genes, identify key biomarkers, and elucidate their underlying mechanisms.

**Methods:**In this study, bulk RNA-seq data and clinical information for LUAD were retrieved from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) and Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) databases, while palmitoylation-related genes were obtained from the GeneCards database. The LUAD gene expression profile was analyzed using DESeq2 and weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA) to identify potential palmitoylation-associated biomarkers. Subsequently, by exploring 117 combinations of 10 machine learning algorithms, a predictive prognostic model based on palmitoylation-related features was constructed. This model was trained on the TCGA cohort (n=493) with 10-fold cross-validation and externally validated using three independent LUAD datasets (GSE30219, GSE72094, and GSE31210). Features independently predictive of prognosis were identified by integrating baseline clinical characteristics and were used to construct a prognostic nomogram. The model’s performance was rigorously evaluated through multi-faceted assessments, including the concordance index (C-index), Kaplan-Meier (KM) survival analysis, time-dependent receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves, and comparative analysis with existing LUAD models from the past year. Core genes were further screened, and their expression patterns and prognostic significance were analyzed. Finally, patients were stratified into high- and low-risk groups based on LUAD palmitoylation-related genes (LPRGs), and differences in genomic alterations, immune microenvironment characteristics, and drug susceptibility between the groups were investigated.

**Results:**Through differential expression analysis, 152 potential candidates were identified. Using machine learning, a prognostic signature comprising 51 LPRGs was constructed. The optimal model, “Random Survival Forest (RSF) + Ridge”, demonstrated a C-index of up to 0.68 in the training set and achieved 0.70 in external validation cohorts. It outperformed other LUAD prognostic models published in the past year across both training and testing datasets. Univariate and multivariate Cox regression analyses confirmed the LPRGs signature and disease stage as independent prognostic predictors, which were subsequently incorporated into a clinical nomogram. A high-risk score was associated with poorer overall survival. Survival analysis indicated that elevated expression of TXN and DNAJB4 was linked to worse outcomes, whereas upregulation of SCN2B, GPD1L and ATP8A2 was correlated with favorable prognosis. Significant disparities were observed between the high- and low-risk groups regarding immune cell infiltration levels, immune functional activity, gene mutation frequency, and anticancer drug susceptibility. High-risk individuals exhibited increased mutation burden, reduced immune infiltration, and a weaker response to immunotherapy. In contrast, the low-risk group demonstrated enhanced drug sensitivity and lower tumor mutational burden.

**Conclusions:**Our work developed a robust LPRGs-based prognostic model and nomogram that personalizes LUAD management and guides therapeutic decisions.

**Keywords:** Lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD); palmitoylation; bioinformatics; machine learning; biomarkers; prognosis

doi: 10.21037/tcr-2025-1389

* * *

Highlight box
-------------

**Key findings**

• A prognostic signature comprising 51 lung adenocarcinoma palmitoylation-related genes (LPRGs) was constructed using a comprehensive machine-learning framework.

**What is known and what is new?**

• Protein palmitoylation is increasingly recognized as a key post-translational modification involved in tumorigenesis and immune regulation in lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD). However, the prognostic significance of LPRGs has not been systematically evaluated.

• This study is the first to develop and externally validate a palmitoylation-based prognostic model using advanced machine-learning strategies, which identified five hub genes (_TXN_, _DNAJB4_, _SCN2B_, _GPD1L_, _ATP8A2_) with bidirectional roles in LUAD progression.

**What is the implication, and what should change now?**

• The LPRG signatures refine LUAD risk stratification and survival prediction, and their integration into clinical workflows can guide immunotherapy decisions. These findings support further mechanistic and translational investigations targeting palmitoylation pathways in LUAD.

Lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) is the predominant subtype of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), originating from bronchial glands or alveolar epithelial cells. The subclassifications include minimally invasive adenocarcinomas (MIA) and invasive mucinous adenocarcinomas (IMA), among others ([1](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B1)). Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide, with LUAD accounting for over 40% of cases ([2](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B2)). It is the most common type of lung cancer among both male and female smokers and non-smokers ([3](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B3)). Molecular heterogeneity includes mutations in various driver genes, such as _EGFR_, _ALK_, _ROS1_, and _KRAS_ ([4](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B4)). Although the application of targeted therapies and immune checkpoint inhibitors has significantly improved the prognosis for certain patients ([5](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B5)), the survival rate remains unsatisfactory, with an overall 5-year survival rate of approximately 15% ([6](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B6)). Tumor heterogeneity, drug resistance, and metastatic recurrence continue to pose significant challenges in clinical treatment.

Over the past decade, the prognostic assessment of LUAD has primarily relied on traditional clinical predictors such as tumor stage, histological grade, age, and gender. With the advancement of precision medicine, personalized treatment strategies have gradually become a research focus. In this context, genomic and transcriptomic analyses have provided critical insights into molecular subtyping and the identification of therapeutic targets for LUAD ([6](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B6),[7](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B7)). However, treatment response and prognosis in LUAD are influenced by multiple factors, including tumor biological characteristics, patient immune status, and the tumor microenvironment (TME) ([8](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B8)). Recently, bioinformatics has highlighted the critical roles of metabolic reprogramming ([7](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B7)), immune evasion ([8](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B8)) and apoptosis ([9](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B9)) in LUAD by integrating data from the genome, epigenome, and proteome. Emerging biological processes such as cuproptosis ([10](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B10)), ribosome biogenesis ([11](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B11)), and ferroptosis ([12](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B12)), which are closely associated with the TME, have also been incorporated into prognostic signatures, offering diverse prognostic guidance for lung cancer treatment. Although the number of predictive model studies has increased in recent years, most models suffer from methodological limitations. These include the use of only a single algorithm for feature selection—leading to the loss of key features and model overfitting—as well as a lack of comparative analysis of algorithm combinations and independent external validation, resulting in insufficient generalizability and inconsistent model quality. Therefore, there is an urgent need to integrate novel prognostic biomarkers with clinical phenotypes to more accurately assess patient outcomes and guide personalized precision therapy ([13](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B13)).

Palmitoylation is a dynamic post-translational modification process that covalently attaches palmitic acid to specific amino acid residues of proteins through thioester bonds. This process can be classified into three categories: S-palmitoylation, N-palmitoylation, and O-palmitoylation ([10](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B10)). The enzymes responsible for catalyzing this reaction, known as palmitoyl transferases (PATs) belong to a family of zinc finger-like proteins that contain DHHC motifs ([14](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B14)). Palmitoylation plays a crucial role in various biological functions, including cell signaling, metabolic reprogramming, and immune regulation, primarily by influencing the membrane localization, stability, and interaction networks of proteins ([15](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B15)). A number of pro-oncogenic proteins and cancer suppressors are shown to undergo palmitoylation and are tightly linked to tumor development ([16](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B16)). On one hand, palmitoylation enhances the sustained activation of pro-survival signaling pathways, such as ERK and PI3K/AKT, by regulating the distribution and localization of pro-oncogenic proteins (e.g., RAS, EGFR) on the lipid membrane ([17](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B17)-[19](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B19)). On the other hand, the oncogenic SCRIB proteins promote the phosphorylation of YAP and TAZ, which consequently inhibit the activation of signaling transduction pathways, such as MAPK, thereby suppressing tumor growth ([20](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B20)). Other signaling transduction pathways, such as Wnt and PD-L1, are also regulated by protein palmitoylation, which affects tumor progression ([21](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B21)). Research has shown that members of the ZDHHC family regulate LUAD progression by targeting key proteins for palmitoylation. For example, ZDHHC21 mediates multisite palmitoylation of EGFR, promoting survival and migration of NSCLC cells; its inhibition enhances EGFR activity ([19](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B19)). ZDHHC9 promotes LUAD proliferation and immune evasion by stabilizing PD-L1 protein levels, while its deletion triggers PD-L1 degradation and activates anti-tumor immunity ([22](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B22)). Knocking down DHHC20, in turn, inhibits the growth of KRAS-mutant LUAD by blocking EGFR palmitoylation and reducing the activity of the PI3K signaling pathway, as well as the stability of the proto-oncoprotein MYC ([23](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#B23)). These findings suggest that the palmitoylation network is not only involved in the pathogenesis of LUAD but may also serve as a crucial target for prognostic markers and drug development.

Research on the prognostic significance of LUAD palmitoylation-related genes (LPRGs) in the TME is still relatively limited. In this study, we systematically explored genes significantly associated with palmitoylation using bulk transcriptomic data from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) and Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) databases. A robust predictive model was constructed by integrating multiple machine learning algorithms, and a clinically applicable nomogram incorporating key clinical features was developed, demonstrating strong predictive performance. During model construction, core candidate genes were identified through diverse computational approaches to further elucidate potential molecular targets related to palmitoylation. Finally, comprehensive stratified analyses—including immune characteristics, mutational landscape, and drug sensitivity—were conducted by categorizing patients into high- and low-risk subgroups. As the first comprehensive study to reveal the multidimensional role of palmitoylation in LUAD, our work establishes a clinically relevant prognostic framework and offers novel therapeutic perspectives for targeted LUAD treatment. We present this article in accordance with the TRIPOD reporting checklist (available at [https://tcr.amegroups.com/article/view/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389/rc](https://tcr.amegroups.com/article/view/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389/rc)).

### Data sources

We obtained RNA sequencing data and clinical information for LUAD from TCGA database ([https://portal.gdc.cancer.gov/](https://portal.gdc.cancer.gov/)). This dataset included raw counts and transcripts per million (TPM) values from 541 LUAD patients and 59 normal tissue samples, which were used for model construction. Corresponding clinical survival data were obtained from UCSC Xena ([http://xena.ucsc.edu/](http://xena.ucsc.edu/)). Patients with incomplete survival information or duplicate samples were excluded from further analysis. The stability and accuracy of the model were evaluated in validation cohorts comprising three datasets from GEO database ([http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/)): GSE30219, GSE72094, and GSE31210. The detailed characteristics of these datasets are summarized in [_Table 1_](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#table1). Palmitoylation-related genes (PRGs) were retrieved from the GeneCards database ([https://www.genecards.org/](https://www.genecards.org/)). Briefly, a gene set was initially acquired by querying the keyword ‘palmitoylation’ in GeneCards. The Relevance Score provided by GeneCards for each gene—a metric that quantifies the strength of evidence linking the gene to the biological process of interest—was used to refine the gene list. To ensure high confidence, we applied a data-driven filtering strategy based on the distribution of relevance scores, ultimately identifying 1,424 PRGs for subsequent analysis. The study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki and its subsequent amendments.

### Identification of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) and the key modular genes by weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA)

Differential gene expressions between tumor and adjacent normal samples in the TCGA dataset were analyzed using the R package DESeq2 (version 1.46.0). DEGs were identified with a threshold of adjusted P value (padj) <0.05 and |log 2 fold change (FC)| >0.585. The ‘ggplot2’ and ‘pheatmap’ packages were employed to generate a volcano plot and a heatmap, respectively, for visualizing the DEGs. To identify key phenotype-related gene modules, WGCNA was performed using the core WGCNA R package (version 1.73). Initially, low-expression genes and outlier samples were filtered out. An appropriate soft-thresholding power (β) was selected to ensure a scale-free topology, with the criterion of a scale-free fit index >0.90 while maintaining a high mean connectivity. The adjacency matrix and topological overlap matrix (TOM) were subsequently calculated. Hierarchical clustering was then applied to group genes into co-expression modules, and a dendrogram with module assignments was constructed. Finally, module-trait associations were evaluated by correlating module eigengenes with clinical traits. Hub genes from modules significantly associated with key phenotypes were extracted as potential key regulators for further analysis.

### Selection of feature hub genes and functional enrichment analysis

The intersection of DEGs, key module genes identified by WGCNA, and PRGs was determined. The resulting overlapping genes were defined as differentially expressed LPRGs and visualized using a Venn diagram. To explore the signaling pathways and biological functions associated with LPRGs, Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway enrichment analyses were performed using the ‘clusterProfiler’ R package.

### Machine learning for development and validation of prognostic LPRGs

After identifying the core LPRGs, we conducted a rigorous data cleaning process on the TCGA database once again. First, we excluded non-tumor samples. Then, we removed duplicate patient IDs from the data and deleted patients with unknown survival status or follow-up duration of less than one month. Lastly, we conducted a complete case analysis without employing any imputation procedures, resulting in a final dataset comprising patients with complete data for both predictor and outcome variables. Prognostic model construction was then performed using the ‘MIME’ package (version 0.0.0.9). The TCGA-LUAD cohort served as the training set, while the GSE30219, GSE72094, and GSE31210 datasets were utilized as external validation cohorts. An initial pool of 152 candidate features was subjected to univariate Cox regression analysis to identify LPRGs with significant prognostic value. Subsequently, the training set was used to conduct ten-fold cross-validation, evaluating a total of 117 algorithm combinations derived from 10 distinct machine learning methods. These included Supervised Principal Components (SuperPC), Random Survival Forest (RSF), Elastic Net (Enet), Stepwise Cox (StepCox), CoxBoost, Partial Least Squares Regression for Cox (plsRcox), Generalized Boosted Regression Modeling (GBM), Survival Support Vector Machine (Survival-SVM), Ridge regression, and least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (Lasso), alongside four potential variable selection filters (Lasso, StepCox, CoxBoost, and RSF). All predictive models were evaluated using Harrell’s C-index across both the training and external validation sets. Model performance was ranked based on the average C-index, leading to the identification of the optimal algorithm combination. The optimal cutoff value for the LPRGs signature was determined using the minimum P value method, stratifying patients into distinct risk subgroups. K-M survival analysis was then performed using the ’survminer’ and ’survival’ R packages, with overall survival (OS) differences between subgroups compared via the log-rank test. Additionally, time-dependent receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves were generated using the ‘timeROC’ R package (version 0.4) to assess the predictive accuracy of the prognostic model at different time points. Simultaneously, we collected signatures of LUAD prediction models published over the past 2 years for comparison with our model, thereby clarifying the clinical benefit assessment of our model.

### Construction and validation of predictive nomogram

Clinical data from the TCGA training cohort were retrieved and summarized in [_Table 2_](https://doi.org/10.21037/tcr-2025-1389#table2). A heatmap was used to visualize the distribution of risk scores and clinical characteristics—including T stage, N stage, sex, and age—across the high- and low-risk subgroups. To further evaluate the prognostic value of the model in a clinical context, univariate and multivariate Cox regression analyses were performed to verify whether the LPRG-based signature served as an independent prognostic factor, distinct from conventional clinical variables such as age, sex, and tumor stage. Subsequently, a clinical prediction nomogram was constructed based on the regression coefficients, integrating survival time, survival status, clinical stage, and risk score into a cumulative point system. This nomogram enables the estimation of individual probabilities of survival outcomes. Developed using the ‘regplot’ package, the nomogram offers an intuitive visualization of prognosis and provides quantifiable survival probability predictions for LUAD patients. To systematically evaluate the predictive performance of this tool, multiple validation approaches were employed: (I) calibration curves were plotted to assess the agreement between predicted probabilities and observed outcomes; (II) time-dependent area under the curve (AUC) analysis was conducted to evaluate the predictive accuracy of the model and individual variables at 1 to 5 years follow-up time points; (III) decision curve analysis (DCA) was performed to quantify the net clinical benefit across a range of threshold probabilities.

### Hub prognostic biomarkers screening

To identify the core variables within the prognostic signature, the ‘MIME’ package was employed to re-analyze the previously identified significant prognostic features using an integrated approach involving eight machine learning algorithms. These included Lasso, Enet, Boruta, CoxBoost, RSF, eXtreme Gradient Boosting (Xgboost), StepCox, and Support Vector Machine-Recursive Feature Elimination (SVM-RFE). Candidate genes were ranked based on their importance, and those with the highest selection frequency were identified as the core PRGs most critically associated with LUAD prognosis. Subsequently, we examined the expression patterns of the five prognostic genes across risk subgroups and evaluated survival outcomes according to their high or low expression levels. Finally, gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) was performed to investigate potential underlying pathways and mechanisms.

### Analysis of tumor mutational burden (TMB) between LPRGs risk groups

We obtained TMB data from TCGA and analyzed the mutational landscape of LUAD patients across different risk subgroups using the maftools package. The results are presented in a waterfall plot displaying the top 20 most frequently mutated genes, while a violin plot is used to compare TMB scores between the two subgroups, serving as a metric for potential response to immunotherapy.

### Immune characteristics and TME analysis

The ‘Immunedeconv’ R package (version 2.1.0) was employed to quantify the association between the palmitoylation modification signature and immune cell infiltration using six advanced algorithms—including QUANTISEQ, TIMER, CIBERSORT, XCELL, MCPCOUNTER, and EPIC—thereby enhancing the robustness of the findings. Furthermore, single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA) was applied to evaluate variations in immune cell characteristics, immune functional activity, and cancer hallmark pathways between LPRG-defined subgroups. The Tumor Immune Dysfunction and Exclusion (TIDE) framework ([http://tide.dfci.harvard.edu](http://tide.dfci.harvard.edu/)) was utilized to assess potential responsiveness to immunotherapy and the likelihood of tumor immune escape. Additionally, the ESTIMATE algorithm was used to compute stromal, immune, ESTIMATE, and tumor purity scores for LUAD patients, enabling the validation of TME heterogeneity across distinct risk subgroups.

### Prediction of drug sensitivity

To identify potential drugs for high-risk patients, we accessed a dataset of drug responses that included 809 tumor cell lines and 198 compounds from the Genomics Database for Cancer Drug Sensitivity (GDSC2, [https://www.cancerrxgene.org/](https://www.cancerrxgene.org/)). Subsequently, we employed the ‘oncoPredict’ R software package (v1.2) to integrate tumor gene expression profiles from TCGA and systematically assess the differences in sensitivity to each type of drug between high- and low-risk groups, based on the drug’s half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC 50) index.

### Statistical analysis

All statistical analyses were conducted using R software (version 4.4.3). A P value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant for all tests.
